coredata.dc:title: A simple and sensitive sensor for lactose based on cascade reactions in Au nanoclusters and enzymes co-encapsulated metal-organic frameworks
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Manli
Chi, Jingtian
Zhang, Chi
Wang, Minglin
Liang, Hui
Hou, Juying
Ai, Shiyun
Li, Xiangyang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, one kind of zeolite imidazole frameworks containing bovine serum albumin stabilized Au nanoclusters (AuNCs), β-galactosidase (β-Gal) and glucose oxidase (GOx) (AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8) were obtained to detect lactose. Compared with other fluorescent nano-materials, AuNCs show distinct advantages as a guest species in ZIF-8, specifically their extremely small size (<1 nm), simple synthesis, excellent biocompatibility and high stability. Furthermore, the bovine serum albumin on their surfaces can promote the formation of ZIF-8 coating; thus, AuNCs were co-encapsulated in ZIF-8 with the enzymes together. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis indicates the composite possesses the similar crystalline structure with pure ZIF-8. Fluorescence microscope images, Fourier transform infrared spectra and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy indicate the presence of AuNCs in the composite. Owing to the high local concentrations of the fluorescent probe and the quenching agent in AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8, the quenching rate was enhanced 3.4-fold that of free AuNCs and enzymes in solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cascade reaction
Fluorescent detection
Metal-organic frameworks
Au nanoclusters
Lactose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of lactose in lactose-free milk drinks
Preparation and characterization of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite
Selectivity and stability of the fluorescence sensor
The detection of lactose in lactose-free milk drinks samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing synthesis method of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 and measuring principle of the sensor for lactose.

(A) SEM image of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8, (B) fluorescence microscope image of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8, (C) XRD images of ZIF-8 and AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8, (D) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8 and AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8.
The influence of (A) Fe2+ ion, (B) the different encapsulation forms of AuNCs and enzymes, (C) pH, and (D) reaction time on the fluorescence spectra in presence of lactose.
(A) Fluorescence spectra in presence of different concentrations of lactose, (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching rate and lactose.
Selectivity and stability of fluorescence sensor for lactose.

head.ce:title: A simple and sensitive sensor for lactose based on cascade reactions in Au nanoclusters and enzymes co-encapsulated metal-organic frameworks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally as first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, one kind of zeolite imidazole frameworks containing bovine serum albumin stabilized Au nanoclusters (AuNCs), β-galactosidase (β-Gal) and glucose oxidase (GOx) (AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8) were obtained to detect lactose. Compared with other fluorescent nano-materials, AuNCs show distinct advantages as a guest species in ZIF-8, specifically their extremely small size (<1 nm), simple synthesis, excellent biocompatibility and high stability. Furthermore, the bovine serum albumin on their surfaces can promote the formation of ZIF-8 coating; thus, AuNCs were co-encapsulated in ZIF-8 with the enzymes together. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis indicates the composite possesses the similar crystalline structure with pure ZIF-8. Fluorescence microscope images, Fourier transform infrared spectra and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy indicate the presence of AuNCs in the composite. Owing to the high local concentrations of the fluorescent probe and the quenching agent in AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8, the quenching rate was enhanced 3.4-fold that of free AuNCs and enzymes in solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Lactose intolerance is a common genotype that accounts of functional lactose deficiency (phenotype) in adulthood (Ingram, Mulcare, Itan, Thomas, & Swallow, 2009; Itan, Jones, Ingram, Swallow, & Thomas, 2010). Up to 65% of the world's population are lactose intolerance (Ugidos-Rodriguez, Matallana-Gonzalez, & Sanchez-Mata, 2018). Lactose intolerance may give rise to flatulence, stomach ache and diarrhea after ingestion of lactose-containing dairy products. (Ugidos-Rodriguez et al., 2018). Lactose-free products have been developed to not only to meet the dietary needs of lactose intolerance populations, specifically for calcium and high-quality protein, but also consumer preferences for intake of these nutrients via dairy-type products. Therefore, there is a demand for easy determination of lactose at low concentrations in lactose-free products (Churakova et al., 2019; Lopez, Ma, Ludwig, Schuhmann, & Ruff, 2017).

At present, there are several techniques available for the determination of lactose, such as gas chromatography, ultra-high-performance chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry and nuclear magnetic resonance (Churakova et al., 2019). However, most of these methods require expensive equipment and complicated sample processes (Das, Sarkar, Rakshit, & Datta, 2018). Fluorescent detection methods are more practical and are popular because of their operational simplicity and speed, sensitivity and selectivity (Chen, Shu, & Wang, 2016; He et al., 2018). The principles of fluorescent detection are based on catalytic reactions of β-galactosidase (β-Gal) and glucose oxidase (GOx). Lactose is first decomposed into galactose and glucose by β-Gal, and then the resulting glucose is decomposed into gluconic acid and H2O2, which cause the quenching reactions. These methods have fatal flaws in widespread applications, such as cost and poor stability.

The zeolite imidazole framework (ZIF-8) recently reveals unique advantages in improving the applicability of enzyme (Wu, Ge, Yang, Hou, & Liu, 2015). On the one hand, ZIF-8 coating can enhance the stability of enzyme remarkably by simple mixing with the precursor of ZIF-8 (Della Rocca, Liu, & Lin, 2011; Liang et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2019). On the other hand, ZIF-8 coating can enhance the catalytic efficiency of cascade reaction in multiple enzymes co-encapsulated ZIF-8 owing to the high local concentrations of the reactants in ZIF-8 (Chen, Vázquez-González, Zoabi, Abu-Reziq, & Willner, 2018). Therefore, ZIF-8 coating has been studied and applied widely in multiple enzymes system (Mehta, Bhardwaj, Bhardwaj, Kim, & Deep, 2016). In this work, we propose that fluorescent Au nanoclusters (AuNCs) and enzymes (β-Gal and GOx) are co-encapsulated in ZIF-8 coating (AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8) (Cui et al., 2016) to enhance the quenching rate of quenching reactant on the fluorescence of AuNCs. Fig. 1 shows the preparation method of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite and the detection principle of lactose. Due to their extremely small size (0.8 nm) and the bovine serum albumin (BSA) on their surface (Liang et al., 2015), BSA stabilized AuNCs can be co-encapsulated with β-Gal and GOx in ZIF-8 coating at room temperature. The composite exhibits an excellent emission peak at about 650 nm at 375 nm excitation. When lactose exists in the system, lactose is first decomposed into galactose and glucose by β-Gal, and then the resulting glucose is decomposed into gluconic acid and H2O2 by GOx in the presence of O2 in the air. The reaction of H2O2 and Fe2+ produces hydroxyl radicals, which lead to the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs (Peng et al., 2019). The detection of lactose in lactose-free milk drinks samples is realized based on the above principles.

Bovine serum albumin, chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), GOx, β-Gal, KH2PO4, K2HPO4, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, HCl, NaOH, lactose, glucose (Glu), mannose (Man), xylosev (Xyl), fructose (Fru), maltose (Mal), ribose (Rib), trehalose (Tre), sucrose (Sur), cellobiose (Cel), melibiose (Mel) and galactose (Gal) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (aladdin, Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used without extra purification. The water used throughout all the experiments was purified through redistillation process.

The morphological characteristics of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan). The elements existed in the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite were confirmed by Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) (Hitachi, Japan). Powder X-ray diffraction data were obtained using Bruker D8 Advance (PANalytical X’Pert, Netherlands). Fluorescence spectra were gained on a Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (VARIAN, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) (Zhengzhou Shuren Technology Development Co., Ltd., Zhengzhou, China) were obtained on a Thermo Nicolet-380 IR spectrophotometer (USA) using the KBr pellet technique over the range 400–4000 cm−1. The fluorescence microscope images of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 were obtained on Nikon Edipse Ti-S (Nikon, Shanghai, China).

Firstly, the AuNCs were prepared according to the previous report (Cao, Li, Yue, & Wu, 2013). HAuCl4 aqueous solution (10 mL, 10 mM) were mixed with BSA aqueous solution (10 mL, 50 mg/mL) at 37 °C under vigorous stirring of magnetic stirrer. After 5 min, NaOH solution (1 mL, 1.0 M) was added. After 24 h under vigorous stirring, the color of the system changed from light yellow to deep brown. Finally, the mixture was dialyzed in ultrapure water for 2 days to remove unreacted impurities. Secondly, the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite was prepared as follows. 1 mL of 2-methylimidazole, 800 µL of AuNCs, 1500 µL of 0.4 mg/mL β-Gal, 1000 µL of 0.5 mg/ml GOx and 1 mL of Zn(Ac)2 were mixed and reacted for 9 h at room temperature. The product was collected through centrifuging at 3000 rpm for 15 min and washing three times with deionized water (Zhu et al., 2019).

Potassium phosphate buffer (10 mM) was used to prepare the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 solution. The pH of buffer was adjusted to the required pH with 0.1 M NaOH or HCl solution. The pH was measured by pH measuring instrument. 0.5 g AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 was dissolved 10 mL potassium phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) to obtain AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 (50 mg/mL) solution. 5 μL lactose solutions with different concentration, 50 µL 0.5 mM of Fe2+ solution, 945 μL of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 (50 mg/mL) solution were mixed and reacted for 15 min in a 37 °C water bath. Then, the fluorescence spectra of the solutions were measured under 375 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of lactose in lactose-free milk drinks
ce:section.ce:para: The determination methods of lactose in lactose-free milk drinks were as follows. The sample was pretreated to eliminate the interference of glucose. 10 μL GOx (10 U/mL) and 1 mL sample were mixed and reacted 25 min at room temperature. And then the mixture was heated to 80 °C for 10 min. Thus, the glucose in milk drinks and the producing H2O2 were decomposed, and the added GOx lost the activity. Lactose in lactose-free samples was detected by the standard addition method. First, different quantities of lactose were added into the milk drinks, and then the solution was filtered through a membrane (0.22 µm) for further use. Second, 5 μL milk drinks, 50 µL 0.5 mM of Fe2+ solution, 945 μL of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 solution (50 mg/mL) was mixed and reacted for 15 min in a 37 °C water bath. Then, the fluorescence spectra of the solutions were measured under 375 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the preparation method of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite and the detection mechanism of lactose. By the biomineralization effect of the β-Gal, GOx and BSA, the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite was obtained by simple mixing of AuNCs, β-Gal, GOx, Zn2+ and 2-methylimidazole at room temperature (Liang et al., 2015). Fig. S1 shows UV–visible absorption, photoemission (λex = 375 nm) and photoexcitation (λem = 650 nm) spectra of of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite. The fluorescence intensity at 650 nm under 375 nm excitation reaches the highest, so the excitation wavelength is fixed at 375 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 650 nm can be quenched by hydroxyl radicals from the following reactions (Peng et al., 2019). Under the catalytic effect of β-Gal/GOx, lactose is first decomposed into galactose and glucose, which can be decomposed into gluconic acid and H2O2. Then, hydroxyl radicals come into being from the Fenton reaction of H2O2 and Fe2+. In order to obtain higher sensitivity, the dosage of AuNCs, β-Gal and GOx in the synthesis reaction of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite was optimized. The optimal dosage of AuNCs was 800 µL according to the highest fluorescence intensity (Fig. S2A). As shown in Figs. S2B and 2C, the dosage of β-Gal and GOx was optimized to 1500 µL and 1000 µL according to the highest fluorescence quenching rate.

The morphology of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite was characterized by scanning electron microscope. As shown in the Fig. 2 A, the composite shows a rod-like structure with the average dimensions of 0.7 × 9.2 µm. Fig. 2C shows XRD pattern of the pure ZIF-8 and AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite. The XRD pattern of the composite is consistent with that of the ZIF-8 indicating that the introducing of nanomaterials has no obvious influence on the crystal structure of ZIF-8 (Ma et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2015). The fluorescence microscope, FT-IR spectra and EDS studies were carried about to testify the existence of AuNCs, β-Gal and GOx in the composite. The fluorescence microscope image of the composite is shown in Fig. 2B. The composite exhibits red under the UV excitation, which indicates the presence of AuNCs. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra of the composite prove that AuNCs, GOx and β-Gal exist in the composite (Fig. 2D). The peak at 1640–1660 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching bands, indicating the existence of BSA, β-Gal and GOx in the composite (Zhu et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1, EDS analysis reveal the existence of C, N, O, S, Zn and Au in the composite.

In order to study the reaction mechanism of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 for the lactose detection, the effect of Fe2+ on the fluorescence quenching was studied. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity decreases to a large extent in presence of Fe2+. The enhancement of quenching degree can be attributed to ·OH from Fenton reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2 (Peng et al., 2019). In contrast to H2O2, ·OH has stronger oxidation ability, and can oxidize Au(0) in the AuNCs into Au(I), thus reduce the fluorescence intensity of the composite (Wang et al., 2018).

The influence of the ZIF-8 coating on the fluorescence quenching rate was discussed. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence quenching rate of (a) AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 system (62.5%) is 3.4 times that of (b) the free AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx system (18.20%). To discuss the reasons for the remarkable enhancement of ZIF-8 coating on the quenching rate, some control experiments were carried out. These experimental results show that the quenching rate for the system of (c) AuNCs and β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 mixture, and (d) AuNCs@ZIF-8, β-Gal@ZIF-8 and GOx@ZIF-8 mixture is 21.84% and 7.52%, respectively. These experimental results indicate that the high fluorescence quenching rate of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 system is attributed to the high local concentration of the intermediate (glucose, H2O2, ·OH) produced in the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 system.

The pH of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 system can lead to the change of the conformation of BSA and enzymes (Xiong et al., 2017), which can affect the fluorescence intensity of composite and the fluorescence quenching rate in presence of lactose. The effect pH of potassium phosphate buffer (10 mM) on the fluorescence quenching rate of lactose was studied. In the pH 7.0 PBS, the quenching efficiency is the highest owing to the high activity of enzymes. The pH system is fixed at 7.0 for the following experiments. Fig. 3D shows that the quenching rate reaches the maximum at 15 min, thus the reaction time is fixed at 15 min.

In order to verify the performance of the fluorescent sensor in detecting lactose, the fluorescence response of the sensor after adding different amounts of lactose was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm decreases gradually with the increase of lactose content. The fluorescence quenching rate (I％) is calculated according to formula (1), where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 solution under 375 nm excitation, F is the fluorescence intensity of solution when lactose and Fe2+ are added.
(1)
I % =
F 0
- F
F 0

Fig. 4B shows that the fluorescence quenching rate varies with the increase of lactose concentration. The result shows that the fluorescence quenching rate has a good linear relationship with the lactose concentration in the range of 125–1000 µM, and the regression equation is described as I = 0.0006c + 0.0024 (R2 = 0.9997), in which c is the lactose concentration in µM. The detection limit at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 was estimated to be 41.67 µM, which was much lower than the threshold (5 g·L−1, 15 mM) of lactose-free milk samples. Table S2 shows the comparison of the different determination methods of lactose, suggesting the sensitivity of this method is higher than that of most previously reported assay systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the selectivity of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite as fluorescent probe for lactose, the effects of other sugars (glucose, mannose, xylose, fructose, maltose, ribose, trehalose, sucrose, cellobiose, melibiose and galactose) on the detection of lactose were studied. Fig. 5 a shows the fluorescence intensity of the composite in presence of lactose (750 µM) and other sugars (1500 µM). Glucose can quench the fluorescence of the system. Concerning the interference of glucose, the real sample should be pretreated with GOx (see Section 2.4). The 1500 µM of other sugars have no great influence on the signals of 750 µM of lactose. The result demonstrates the high selectivity of the sensing system of lactose. The long-term storage stability of enzymes is vital for the practicability of sensor. The relative activity of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 and the free enzyme system after long-term storage in PBS 7.4 at room temperature was calculated according to the relative fluorescence quenching rate (Fig. 5b). After storage of 3 days, the relative activity of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 and the free enzyme systems is 76.83% and 5.30% of initial overall activity, respectively. The stability improvement of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 against long-term storage is owing to the protection effect of the ZIF-8 coating on the enzymes. The stability of enzymes against digestive enzymes and chelating compounds is also important for the glucose detection in the biological system. Trypsin and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) is chosen as the model of digestive enzymes and chelating compounds (Fig. 5c). After trypsinization for 30 min at 37 °C, the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 and the free enzyme system lost 6.85% and 31.67% of initial overall activity, respectively. These results demonstrate that the ZIF-8 coating endows the composite with good tolerance to digestive enzymes. The relative activity of the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite and free enzymes system in the presence of 1 wt% EDTA is 73.47% and 16.13%, respectively. These results indicate that the ZIF-8 coating can improve the stability of the system against chelating compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of lactose in lactose-free milk drinks samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the applicability and practicability of the fluorescence sensor, lactose in lactose-free milk beverage samples was detected by the standard addition method. As can be seen from Table S3, the recovery of lactose ranges from 98.65% to 105.42%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 3% (n = 5). These results indicate that the fluorescence sensor displays good practicability in detecting lactose-free milk drinks samples.

In conclusion, the AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 composite as the fluorescent sensor for lactose was obtained at room temperature. The composite exhibits excellent emission at 650 nm, which can be quenched by hydroxyl radicals from lactose. Owing to the local high concentration of reaction reagents in ZIF-8, the quenching rate of lactose on the fluorescence of AuNCs/β-Gal/GOx@ZIF-8 increases to 3.4 times that of the free AuNCs and enzymes. These results indicate that ZIF-8 coating possesses wide application prospects in fluorescence analysis.

Manli Guo: Conceptualization. Jingtian Chi: Writing - original draft. Chi Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Minglin Wang: Data curation. Hui Liang: Data curation. Juying Hou: Writing - review & editing. Shiyun Ai: Conceptualization. Xiangyang Li: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872889, 41771342), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2019MD025), the Key R&D Program of Shandong Province, China (2018YYSP029), the “13th Five-Year” National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFD0801504, 2017YFF0211300) and the Funds of Shandong Double Tops Program, China (SYL2017XTTD01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127863.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
